message:
docs(readme): add row_multiindex + column_multiindex to DataFrame ASCII tree (br-gnzcw / fd90.96)

fd90.96: same omission pattern as fd90.95 but for DataFrame. The Data
Model ASCII tree at lines 244-246 listed 3 fields, but
crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs:14733 actually declares 5:

  pub struct DataFrame {
      index: Index,
      row_multiindex: Option<fp_index::MultiIndex>,    <- WAS MISSING
      columns: BTreeMap<String, Column>,
      column_order: Vec<String>,
      column_multiindex: Option<fp_index::MultiIndex>, <- WAS MISSING
  }

Hiding the MultiIndex fields contradicts the README's own row/col
MultiIndex coverage advertised at lines 34 and 424.

Fix: add both fields with annotations describing where they get set
(set_index_multi for rows; column header path for columns).

Pure README change. No code impact.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"frankenpandas-gm3r","title":"fd90.30: SqlConnection::list_views for backend-agnostic view discovery","description":"Add SqlConnection::list_views(schema: Option<&str>) -> Result<Vec<String>, IoError> trait method completing the SQLAlchemy.Inspector.get_view_names() parity. Default impl returns Ok(Vec::new()). rusqlite override queries sqlite_master WHERE type='view' (excluding sqlite_*). Multi-schema backends override with information_schema.views filter. Free-fn list_sql_views wrapper. Tests cover empty DB, populated views, table-vs-view separation (list_tables doesn't surface views and vice versa), schema-Some on SQLite (silently ignored), default impl returns empty, multi-schema stub routing.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","assignee":"cc-pandas-2","created_at":"2026-04-25T21:13:57.344071387Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-25T21:16:51.595524278Z","closed_at":"2026-04-25T21:16:51.592965131Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["fd90","review","sql"]}
 {"id":"frankenpandas-gnjt","title":"fp-columnar ValidityMask.slice/concat parity","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T08:24:54.280108818Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-21T08:28:28.173581351Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T08:28:28.171747578Z","close_reason":"Implemented ValidityMask::slice/concat in commit 29b76f1, 3 tests","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
+{"id":"frankenpandas-gnzcw","title":"fd90.96: README DataFrame ASCII tree omits row_multiindex + column_multiindex fields","description":"fd90.74-95 README audit. Same pattern as fd90.95 but for DataFrame. The Data Model ASCII tree at lines 244-246 shows DataFrame with 3 fields (index, columns, column_order) but crates/fp-frame/src/lib.rs:14733 actually declares 5 fields:

  pub struct DataFrame {
      index: Index,
      row_multiindex: Option<fp_index::MultiIndex>,    <- MISSING from README
      columns: BTreeMap<String, Column>,
      column_order: Vec<String>,
      column_multiindex: Option<fp_index::MultiIndex>, <- MISSING from README
  }

The MultiIndex fields are central to the row/column hierarchical indexing feature that the README itself elsewhere advertises (line 34: 'Row MultiIndex Yes', line 424: 'DataFrame integration now carries a logical row MultiIndex alongside the flat storage index'). Hiding them from the data-model diagram is misleading.

Fix: add the two row_multiindex / column_multiindex fields with annotations. Pure README change.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-26T09:31:25.443773432Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-26T09:32:37.222845619Z","closed_at":"2026-04-26T09:32:37.220175813Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"frankenpandas-gpxk","title":"[HIGH] fuzz: 6 pub SQL-reading fns in fp-io lack fuzz targets — crafted query strings hit driver unsafety undetected","description":"Applying /testing-fuzzing skill decision tree: "Database engine → STATEFUL + GRAMMAR-BASED".

**Observation.** fp-io exposes SQL surfaces that accept query strings:

  read_sql<C: SqlConnection>(conn, query: &str)            → fp-io/src/lib.rs:3302
  read_sql_with_options<C: SqlConnection>(conn, ..., options) → fp-io/src/lib.rs:3309
  read_sql_with_index_col                                  → fp-io/src/lib.rs:3360
  read_sql_table<C: SqlConnection>(conn, table_name: &str) → fp-io/src/lib.rs:3429
  read_sql_table_with_index_col                           → fp-io/src/lib.rs:3375
  read_sql_table_columns                                   → fp-io/src/lib.rs:3448

(Note: The SqlConnection trait already exists — br-frankenpandas-fd90's slice 1 or a parallel agent's work on that epic has landed. Still, the strings that flow to the backend are untrusted user input.)

None of these six pub fns have any fuzz target. Input strings flow directly into rusqlite / (future: tokio-postgres / mysql_async) prepared-statement handling. A crafted query could:
- Panic the driver on an unterminated statement.
- OOM via an expression that allocates an enormous result buffer.
- Bypass our layer's own validation to trigger a backend-side bug.
- Hit the column-name → Scalar::* translator with an unexpected SQLite type (e.g. BLOB, NULL with PRAGMA type_affinity off) and crash the downstream DataFrame builder.

**Skill rule invoked:** Rule 3 (fuzz every untrusted-input pub fn) + the "Database engine" branch of the decision tree. The decision-tree recommendation is STATEFUL + GRAMMAR-BASED: generate random-but-valid SQL, run it against a shadow model (an in-memory SQLite with known state).

**Severity.** HIGH — SQL is the highest-risk IO path we expose. Even with `#![forbid(unsafe_code)]` on our side, the backend drivers (rusqlite's C-interop for SQLite, future PG/MySQL libs) are unsafe-rich code. A crafted query that exercises an untested path can trigger backend-level UB; our layer's coverage of that path determines whether we detect it or silently forward the result.

**Fix shape:**

Phase 1 — minimum viable fuzz target (crash detector):
1. `fuzz/fuzz_targets/fuzz_sql_read.rs` — wraps `fp_conformance::fuzz_read_sql_bytes`.
2. `fp-conformance::fuzz_read_sql_bytes(data: &[u8]) -> Result<(), String>`:
   - Set up an in-memory rusqlite::Connection with a fixed schema (say, 3 tables: `t1(a INT, b TEXT)`, `t2(k TEXT PRIMARY KEY, v REAL)`, `empty_t()`) populated with 10-20 deterministic rows.
   - Convert data bytes to UTF-8 lossy, clamp to 4096 chars.
   - Call `read_sql(&conn, &query_str)`. Expect Ok or Err; assert no panic.
   - If Ok, assert the returned DataFrame's column count matches the query's SELECT list arity (via a cheap regex on the query string — only for statements that parse as SELECT).

Phase 2 — structure-aware grammar fuzz:
3. Use `SQLsmith` or a small hand-grammar that generates syntactically valid SQL against the fixture schema. Feeds the grammar's output to the same fuzz_read_sql_bytes harness. Catches deeper code paths (JOIN, GROUP BY, subquery, UNION, window functions) that random bytes would never produce.

Phase 3 — STATEFUL:
4. `fuzz_sql_session_bytes` — byte input parsed as a sequence of SQL statements (CREATE, INSERT, UPDATE, SELECT, DELETE). Run each against a rusqlite connection + a shadow BTreeMap model. After each statement, assert the connection's state equals the shadow model's state. Detects state-corruption bugs that single-query fuzz misses.

5. Eventually (once br-fd90 PostgreSQL backend lands): differential fuzz — same grammar-generated SQL against rusqlite AND tokio-postgres, assert same result DataFrame. Catches backend-specific divergence.

Seed corpus (5 minimum for Phase 1):
- empty string
- `SELECT 1` (trivial valid)
- `SELECT * FROM t1` (schema-referencing)
- `SELECT * FROM nonexistent_table` (error path)
- `DROP TABLE t1; SELECT * FROM t1` (multi-statement — likely rejected, assert no panic)

**Related:**
- br-fd90 (SQL backend epic) — slice 7 (live-oracle conformance per backend) naturally overlaps with Phase 2 grammar fuzz.
- br-zjme (fuzz CI) — adds new targets to the existing regression gate.
- br-liai (differential vs pandas) — pandas's `pd.read_sql(query, engine)` can serve as oracle for Phase 2 fuzz.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"cc-pandas","created_at":"2026-04-22T20:49:05.843007879Z","created_by":"cc-pandas","updated_at":"2026-04-23T17:17:53.360013202Z","closed_at":"2026-04-23T17:17:53.357292241Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["fp-io","fuzz","review","sql"]}
 {"id":"frankenpandas-gsq6","title":"fp-index Index.asof (nearest-preceding lookup) parity","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-21T08:49:22.296852910Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-21T08:58:10.959292697Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T08:58:10.957556580Z","close_reason":"Implemented Index::asof in commit b17e94a, 2 tests","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"frankenpandas-gsrv","title":"fd90.78: refine DISC-012 root cause - mixed-tz CSV parse bails not format string mismatch","description":"fd90.77 documented DISC-012 as a 'datetime tz format string' divergence. Deeper investigation: it's actually a parse-failure root cause - parse_csv_datetime_column's parse_failed guard returns None when ANY row in a mixed naive/aware tz column doesn't parse, leaving the entire column as raw input strings. Row 0 (naive '2024-01-15 10:30:00') parses fine. Row 1 ('2024-01-15T10:30:00Z') with tz suffix gets NaT under the Naive pattern (the inferrer picks the first row's pattern). The combined parse_failed=true flag bails the whole column. Pandas handles mixed-tz by parsing rows independently. Update DISC-012 to reflect the corrected root cause and link the eventual fix bead to to_datetime_with_options independent-row parsing in fp-frame.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"docs","assignee":"cc-pandas-2","created_at":"2026-04-26T07:42:19.644220421Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-26T07:44:10.968516260Z","closed_at":"2026-04-26T07:44:10.965893395Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["discrepancy","docs","fd90","review"]}

@@ -241,9 +241,11 @@ FrankenPandas uses a columnar storage model identical to pandas' internal repres
 
 ```
 DataFrame
-├── index: Index (Vec<IndexLabel>)  ← Row labels (Int64/Utf8/Datetime64/Timedelta64)
-├── columns: BTreeMap<String, Column>  ← Named columns
-└── column_order: Vec<String>  ← Insertion-order preservation
+├── index: Index (Vec<IndexLabel>)        ← Row labels (Int64/Utf8/Datetime64/Timedelta64)
+├── row_multiindex: Option<MultiIndex>    ← Optional hierarchical row index (set by set_index_multi)
+├── columns: BTreeMap<String, Column>     ← Named columns
+├── column_order: Vec<String>             ← Insertion-order preservation
+└── column_multiindex: Option<MultiIndex> ← Optional hierarchical column header
 
 Column
 ├── dtype: DType  ← {Null, Bool, Int64, Float64, Utf8, Categorical, Timedelta64, Sparse}
